solution.p: We will factor 2t^{2}-11t+14 where a=2, b=-11, and c=14.
AC Method: In this method, we first calculate the product of a and c in ax^2+bx+c. For this problem, we have (2) \cdot (14) = 28.

The number 28 can be factored into the product of two numbers in the following ways:  egin{array}{lll}1(28)\amp -1(-28)\amp 2(14)\amp -2(-14)
ewline4(7)\amp -4(-7)\amp \end{array} Looking at the corresponding sums, we want a sum of -11, and this sum happens with (-4)+(-7)=-11 So we write  egin{aligned}[t]2t^{2}-11t+14\amp =2t^{2}-4t-7t+14
ewline \amp =2t\mathopen{}\left(t-2ight)-7\mathopen{}\left(t-2ight)
ewline \amp =\left(2t-7ight)\mathopen{}\left(t-2ight)\end{aligned}Generic Rectangles Method: First, we set up generic rectangles by putting the first term and third term in 2t^{2}-11t+14 into the top left and bottom right rectangles:
The first term's coefficient, 2, can be factored into the product of two numbers (where the first factor is positive) in the following ways: \begin{array}{llll}1(2)\amp \end{array}
The third term, 14, can be factored into the product of two numbers in the following ways: \begin{array}{llll}1(14)\amp -1(-14)\amp 2(7)\amp -2(-7)\newline\end{array}

We put each pair into the corresponding places next to those generic rectangles, and try to match the area of those rectangles with 2t^{2}-11t+14 by guess-and-check. The following generic rectangles show the solution:
Dimensions of those generic rectangles in the diagram give us the solution: \begin{aligned}[t]2t^{2}-11t+14\amp =\left(2t-7\right)\mathopen{}\left(t-2\right)\end{aligned}
